metadata.title: Why R² Is a Broken Compass for Model Size

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: You have 20 predictors. You use R-squared to choose how many to include. Adding any predictor — even a random one — always increases R-squared. So the best model by R-squared always has all 20 predictors. That is not model selection. That is a trap.

card.copy: Why R² Is a Broken Compass for Model Size

build.suggested: suggested prompt: You have 20 predictors. You use R-squared to choose how many to include. Adding any predictor — even a random one — always increases R-squared. So the best mode

beats.narration_text: R-squared measures how much variance in the response is explained by the model. Adding a predictor can only increase this — it gives the model more information to fit the training data. R-squared is monotonically non-decreasing with predictors.

production_viz.label: R² vs number-of-predictors: monotone increasing curve with 'TRAP' label at peak — always goes up

beats.narration_text: Why can't you use R-squared or training error to choose how many predictors to include — and what is the computationally feasible alternative to testing all 2^P subsets?

card.copy: R² always goes up when you add a predictor. Why does that make it useless for model selection — and what actually works?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Why can't you use R-squared or training error to choose how many predictors to include — and what is the computationally feasible alternative to testing all 2^P

beats.narration_text: The problem is overfitting. More predictors fits training data better — but may be fitting noise rather than signal. On new test data, the overfitted model performs worse. R-squared measures training fit, not generalization.

production_viz.label: Training vs test error curves: training decreases monotonically, test error has a minimum — sweet spot

beats.narration_text: Expected test error decomposes as bias squared plus variance plus irreducible noise. Adding predictors reduces bias but increases variance. There is a sweet spot — a number of predictors that minimizes test error. R-squared can't find it.

production_viz.label: Bias-variance tradeoff: bias² + variance + noise = test error, sweet spot at optimal predictor count

beats.narration_text: Testing all possible subsets: with p predictors, there are 2 to the p subsets. At p equals 10: 1024 — feasible. At p equals 40: over a trillion — infeasible. Best-subset selection breaks down above p around 40.

production_viz.label: 2^P subset explosion: 2^10=1024 (feasible), 2^40>1 trillion (infeasible) — best subset breaks above P≈40

beats.narration_text: Backward stepwise selection: start with the full model. At each step, remove the predictor that causes the smallest decrease in model fit — the least useful predictor. This generates p plus 1 candidate models.

production_viz.label: Backward stepwise: full model → remove least useful predictor at each step → P+1 candidate models

beats.narration_text: P plus 1 candidates instead of 2 to the p: at p equals 20, that is 21 models instead of over a million. At p equals 50: 51 models instead of 10 to the 15. Backward stepwise is greedy but computationally feasible.

production_viz.label: Comparison: P=20: 21 backward stepwise vs 1,048,576 best subset; P=50: 51 vs 10^15

beats.narration_text: Then use test error to choose among the p plus 1 candidates. Cross-validation, Akaike Information Criterion, or Bayesian Information Criterion all estimate test error and penalize model complexity explicitly.

production_viz.label: P+1 candidates evaluated by CV / AIC / BIC — test error estimate selects winner among candidates

beats.narration_text: R-squared always increases with predictors — useless for model selection. Best subset: 2 to the p models, infeasible above p around 40. Backward stepwise: p plus 1 candidates by greedily removing the least useful predictor. Then cross-validation or AIC/BIC to choose the winner.

card.copy: R² monotone — can't choose model size. Best subset: 2^P (infeasible above P≈40). Backward stepwise: P+1 candidates. Then CV/AIC/BIC to pick. P+1 comparisons instead of 2^P.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: R-squared always increases with predictors — useless for model selection. Best subset: 2 to the p models, infeasible above p around 40. Backward stepwise: p plu

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
